heading: CORRISPONDONO ALL'ARCHETIPO
card_summary: This long section of the De Harmonia Mundi (at least fifteen chapters) systematically demonstrates that every order of created reality — from prime matter and the four elements through stones, metals, plants, mountains, plains, deserts, fertile earth, seas, springs, rivers, and lakes — possesses a counterpart in the divine Archetype. Zorzi grounds the argument in Augustine's doctrine of number, weight, and measure (Wisdom 11:20), the Kabbalistic sefirot (citing the Sefer Yetzirah attributed to Abraham, and the term Shekinah), and a sustained series of scriptural proofs drawn from Ecclesiasticus, the Psalms, Ezekiel, Job, Zechariah, and the Pauline epistles. The four elements are mapped onto the Persons of the Trinity and the Logos: water corresponds to the Son, fire to the Holy Spirit, earth to the Father, and air (following the Sefer Yetzirah) to the Spirit's unifying function. Philosophical authorities — Eusebius of Caesarea, Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, Plato, Aristotle, Thales, Zeno of Citium, Timaeus of Locri, Empedocles, Hermes Trismegistus, and Plutarch — are marshalled alongside patristic sources. The Hebrew terms din (strict divine judgment), Shekinah, even (stone, whose letters encode Av/Father and Ben/Son), and the sefirot's dimensiones and annumerationes are introduced as Kabbalistic confirmation of Christian trinitarian cosmology.
essay:
# CORRISPONDONO ALL'ARCHETIPO — Scholarly Summary

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 59 (chapters I–V in representative detail, with chapter headings noted through chapter XV). The full section runs to approximately 30,800 words across pages 901–979.*

## Chapter I: All Things Have a Counterpart in the Archetype (p. 901)

Zorzi opens with the foundational claim that every created reality pre-existed in its Maker, just as a building pre-exists in the architect's mind. The controlling proof-text is Wisdom 11:20 (number, weight, and measure), interpreted through Augustine: the norms by which things are measured cannot themselves be among the measured things, and since nothing exists outside God and creatures, they must reside in God. Zorzi immediately fuses this with Kabbalistic doctrine, attributing the first disclosure of the Archetype's inner *dimensiones* and *annumerationes* to the patriarch Abraham — a clear reference to the *Sefer Yetzirah*:

> «In Dio vi sono numeri senza numero» (citando Agostino), «poiché tutte le cose che sono in Lui, coincidono con l'unico Dio.»
<cite>p. 901 (Italian text)</cite>

The scriptural catena that follows is extensive: Ecclesiasticus on counting sand, raindrops, and the days of the age; Psalm 147 (God counts and names the stars); the Gospel saying that God numbers the hairs of the head; Ezekiel's measuring reed (six cubits and a palm); the golden reed of Revelation; Daniel to Belshazzar (*Mane Thecel Phares*); Job on the numbered months of human life; Zechariah's measuring of Jerusalem. Paul's letters to the Romans and Ephesians supply the idea that faith, grace, and even the resurrection body are distributed *secundum mensuram*.

The Hebrew term *din* (strict justice, the divine judgment-measure) is introduced and glossed:

> «detta dagli ebrei din, che significa giudizio severo e forte, tanto che nessuno può uscirne giustificato»
<cite>p. 901 (Italian text, treating the dual measure of justice)</cite>

A dual divine measure — a greater (montane, *din*) and a lesser — structures moral accounting. The chapter closes by announcing a descent to particular correspondences.

## Chapter II: Prime Matter Corresponds to the Omnipotent Father (p. 901–902)

Zorzi argues that pure passive potency — prime matter — corresponds to God the Father, the first Act. He cites Eusebius of Caesarea (*De Triade et Monade*) on the eternal opposition of first Ens and matter: the former only acts, the latter only receives. The physical vocabulary of matter is surveyed: *pandoche* (from some natural philosophers), *sylva* / forest (Pythagoras, Empedocles, and the Peripatetics), and Plato's *sinus informis*, mother-of-the-world, receptacle, and nurse of forms (*Timaeus*).

Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite is cited for the apophatic method that governs both extremes of the ontological spectrum: neither the first Ens nor pure matter is graspable by positive predication. Zeno of Citium's two principles (God as active cause, matter as passive) are noted, and Plato and Socrates add the third — the Idea in the divine mind or Word. Moses's narrative in Genesis is read as preserving the mystery that matter was created directly by the Father without intermediate cause, whereas all other things were created through mediating agents:

> «la creazione della materia e la stessa materia creata differiscono dalla creazione del resto e dalle cose stesse venute alla luce a tal punto che Timeo di Locri e Platone giudicarono impura la speculazione sulla materia»
<cite>p. 902 (Italian text)</cite>

## Chapter III: Earth Corresponds to the Father (p. 902–903)

Earth, as the centre of the physical universe and the stable receptacle of all fertility, corresponds to the Father as the immovable centre of the intellectual sphere. The Father generates two shoots: the Son and the Holy Spirit. Isaiah's verse *Aperiatur terra, et germinet salvatorem* is given a triple interpretation: the Virgin Mary (Christian theologians), the Shekinah — glossed as *la dimora di Dio in noi mediata e familiare* (Jewish Kabbalists) — and the paternal mind itself (Zorzi's own preferred reading). The Psalm verse *Veritas de terra orta est* is applied to the Son as Word and Truth. Plutarch's testimony that physicists saw God beginning creation from earth as the centre and mother of all things is adduced. Earth's eternal immobility (in contrast to the mobile elements) mirrors the Father's unchanging self-identity even as the Son, Holy Spirit, and angels are sent outward.

## Chapter IV: The Remaining Elements Resonate with the Archetype (p. 903–906)

This is the most elaborate elemental allegory in the opening portion.

**Water / Son.** Water carries seminal power (as Thales recognised, according to Zorzi, when he named water the principle of all things). The Son as Logos is the true water: *Omnia per verbum facta sunt* (John 1:3). Hermes Trismegistus is cited on the need for seeds to become liquid (*tabefieri*) before they can germinate — a process that originates in the divine fountain of ideas. Zorzi elaborates a chain of water-images all applied to Christ: the water given to Nicodemus for spiritual rebirth, the living water promised to the Samaritan woman (John 4), the water distilled through the Vine that becomes the wine of the Eucharist, and the purifying water with which the Son washes sin (Revelation 1:5, Zechariah 13:1, Hebrews 1:3).

> «Chiunque beva dell'acqua che io gli darò diverrà in lui una sorgente di acqua che scaturisce in vita eterna»
<cite>p. 904 (Italian text, citing John 4)</cite>

**Fire / Holy Spirit.** The Holy Spirit corresponds to fire: it cooks, purifies gold, fuses disparate metals, and transmutes natures. Zorzi applies this to specific transformations — David the shepherd-harpist into the supreme prophet, fishermen into doctors of the gentiles, Saul the persecutor into Paul the vessel of election. Isaiah 4:4 provides the phrase *spiritus iudicii* and *spiritus ardoris* (the dual operation of Son and Spirit on the elect soul).

**Air / the Spirit's unifying power.** Drawing explicitly on the *Sefer Yetzirah* attributed to Abraham (*libro de formatione*), Zorzi notes that air is not properly an element but a medium, glue, and bond holding diverse things together — and so corresponds to the Spirit's unifying function. He is careful to forestall the objection that two Holy Spirits are being posited; the air-correspondence names only one attribute of the single Spirit.

> «l'aria corrisponde alla potenza dello Spirito, in virtù della quale esso unisce tutte le cose che sembrano contrapposte»
<cite>p. 906 (Italian text)</cite>

Ezekiel 1:20 (*spiritus vitae erat in rotis*) and 1 Corinthians 12:11 (*idem spiritus dividit singulis*) seal the argument.

## Chapter V: Stones Correspond to Father and Son (p. 906–907)

Stones, being earthy and suited for foundations, correspond to both Father and Son. The Hebrew word *even* (אבן, stone) is subjected to a classic Kabbalistic letter-analysis: its first two letters form *av* (אב, Father) and its middle-plus-final letters form *ben* (בן, Son). This makes the stone an emblem of the trinitarian bond between Father and Son. Christ as the cornerstone rejected by the builders (Psalm 118, Matthew 21), as the foundation-stone (*nemo potest ponere aliud fundamentum*, 1 Cor 3:11), and as the rock struck by Moses from which waters flowed for Israel (1 Cor 10:4) are all expounded. The Father is identified with the Hebrew *tsur* (צור), rendered as *fortis* / rock of refuge and the mountain-strong one — a divine name that properly belongs to omnipotent paternal power.

## Subsequent Chapters (VI–XV): Outline

The following chapter titles indicate the scope of the remainder of the section, which this summary does not cover in detail:
- **Caput VI** — Metals and their archetypes
- **Caput VII** — Plants and their archetypes
- **Caput VIII** — Plains (*planities*)
- **Caput IX** — Mountains
- **Caput X** — Deserts and arid places
- **Caput XI** — Fertile earth
- **Caput XII** — Sea and waters
- **Caput XIII** — Springs
- **Caput XIV** — Rivers and channels
- **Caput XV** — Pools, lakes, and ponds

The method throughout is consistent with the opening chapters: each natural category is shown to possess a precise counterpart in the Archetype by triangulating scriptural typology, philosophical cosmology (Platonic, Peripatetic, Stoic), and Kabbalistic number-speculation.

## Zorzi's Cosmological Project

This section is the culminating systematic table of correspondences in the *De Harmonia Mundi*. Having established in earlier toni that the elements resonate in the celestial spheres and among the angels, Zorzi now completes the chain by grounding everything in the Archetype itself. The Kabbalistic *sefirot* supply the structural nodes of the Archetype; the Augustinian doctrine of measure, number, and weight provides the Christian-philosophical frame; and the Neoplatonic hierarchy of first Act / pure potency / intermediaries (drawn from Eusebius, Proclus via Dionysius, and Plato's *Timaeus*) furnishes the metaphysical architecture. The result is a comprehensive *harmonia* in which every physical, geological, and hydrological feature of the created world is a resonant mirror of divine trinitarian reality.
